title: Let's learn about the basic concepts of database part 1
description: Database design is so difficult that when I tried to organize it, I realized that I don't know anything about database basics. So, before I start w...

children.value:
Database design is so difficult that when I tried to organize it, I realized that I don't know anything about database basics.
So, before I start writing the Database Design series, I'm going to summarize the Getting Started with Databases series.
In this article, I'll summarize the information you need to get started with databases.

Consider a social networking service as an example.
Users post photos, write posts, comment on other users' content, and more.
As such, data that has the same origin and purpose within the same service is called related data.

Imagine that your books are strewn across the floor.
It's hard to find them quickly, and it's hard to tell if you have duplicates of the same book.
But if you organize them and put them on a bookshelf, you can quickly find the book you want and easily see if you have unnecessary duplicates.
A set that organizes data in this way to increase efficiency and data accessibility is called an organized set.

It means storing data efficiently and keeping it on a computer where it can be quickly retrieved.
This allows for fast computation and retrieval while taking up little space.
Software that provides users with the ability to

, create, and manage databases, most commonly PostgreSQL, MySQL, Oracle, and MsSQL.
In the process of defining a database, the structure of the data is determined, and this process generates additional data, which is called metadata.
The term is widely used in a variety of fields, and its original meaning is "

," meaning that it refers to data that describes or characterizes data.
For example, when you take a photo, you may see additional information that describes the photo, such as the date it was taken, the file format, the resolution, etc.

In the database world, it refers to the data that defines or describes a database, which is sometimes referred to as a catalog.
The catalog can refer to the metadata itself, but it can also refer to the place where the metadata is stored and managed by the DBMS.
Examples of metadata include data types, structures, constraints, security, storage, indexes, and user groups.

it refers to the Database + DBMS + associated applications.
However, if you read several documents, you may shorten the database system and call it just database.
Therefore, it is necessary to understand the meaning by carefully grasping the context of whether the database of the document means pure data or database system.
Users / Programmers: The application is used by users or programmers.

Application Programs / Queries: The application sends queries to the DBMS to perform some function requested by the user.
In this context, a query is a request to get the desired data from the database, or to modify or delete it in a desired form.
Software to Process Queries / Programs: Analyze what the query means.
Software to Access Stored Data: Once it understands what is being requested, it processes the request.
Stored Database Definition: DBMS needs to know the metadata before we can process the data, so we find and verify the metadata first.
Stored Database: Process the desired data based on the metadata and return the results to the application.

A set of concepts that can be used to describe the structure of a database.
Based on those concepts, it provides a way to

the structure of a database and represent it in a desired form.
The data model also includes the basic operations for reading and writing to the database.
The database structure mentioned refers to concepts that include data types, relationships between data, constraints, etc.
There are several types of data models, each with a slightly different level of abstraction and a different way of structuring the database.

It is a model consisting of concepts that can be easily understood by not only developers but also ordinary users, and has the highest level of abstraction.
This data model is used to describe business requirements at an abstract level.
A representative data model is the
, and its visual representation is the Entity-Relationship Diagram, or ER Diagram for short.
The figure below shows an ER diagram of the relationship between a student and a book, assuming you are building a university library system.

It provides concepts that allow you to structure your database in a way that is not too difficult to understand.
Structuring the database in detail means structuring the logical data model so that it is not significantly different from the structure of the data model stored on the computer.
However, this model does not even represent how data is stored on a physical storage device, but rather is a model that can structure the database at a level that is not dependent on a specific DBMS or storage.

The main data model is the relational data model, and other data models include the object data model and the object-relational data model.
Among the major DBMSs mentioned above, Oracle, MySQL, and MsSQL are based on the relational data model.
And PostgreSQL is based on an object-relational data model but includes a relational data model, so it can be said that the major DBMSs are based on a relational data model.
series, we'll focus on this relational data model.

As can be seen in the figure above, relationship simply means a table.
In a table, row means one data and is called a tuple or record.
In addition, a column is called an attribute and refers to the characteristics of a tuple or record.

It provides a way to describe how data is stored in files on a computer.
In other words, it is a data model that expresses elements such as data format, data orderings, and access paths to represent the database structure as closely as possible to the way data is actually stored on the storage device.
Among them, access paths are structures to speed up data retrieval, such as indexes.

should also be applied to tests.

attribute, this attribute is for filtering out component classes that are not to be registered as beans.

In this example, we're excluding Flyway from the autoconfiguration.

children.value:
It provides an environment to test the controller without running the actual server.
To simulate a request, you call the Dispatcher Servlet directly using

containing the request information as a parameter.
Let's take a look at the example below to get a rough idea of the flow of operations when testing the controller.
Lines 7-9 : Configure the servlet environment for testing through
to send a request to the controller.
As explained earlier, since we are not starting a live server, we call the
directly and communicate with the mocked request and response objects.
Lines 16-17 : Validate the results with the mock response returned by
To illustrate the flow of operations, the example above uses a manual initialization of

without using Spring Context.
There are other ways to initialize it directly using Web Application Context or using Spring Boot auto-configuration.
To get a better understanding of
, let's take a look at how to initialize it.
Below is a common configuration that will be used in this part.

instance, and it does not use the Spring Context, but stubbing the Web Application Context.
Therefore, it can not use the features of the Spring Framework.

Because we're using a Spring Context, we can use the features of the Spring Framework.

that should be injected on line 9.

annotation to create the actual Spring Context.

that I outlined in the previous post, it seems like I should be able to find a class with the

attribute and register all of component classes as beans just like a real Spring Context.
However, if you don't explicitly specify the
attribute, no Spring Context is created.
It's declared at the class level, and spring boot will automatically initialize

and register it as a bean.
We can inject and use it at our convenience.

.
This builder can be created through static factory methods, of which there are the following types
Create a builder for GET requests.
Create a builder for POST requests.

Create a builder for delete requests.

Create a builder for multipart requests.
Create a RequestBuilder that has started asynchronous processing.
To use it, you can pass a URI or a string as a parameter.
Note that if you pass a string as a parameter, it will be substituted in the order of the

children.value: In this article, we will look at the annotations that you basically need to know when testing using spring boots, and the annotations that help you understand other annotations.
It is used to load and configure ApplicationContext for integration testing, and
attribute is usually used to specify
// ApplicationContext will be loaded from FooConfig and BarConfig

✅ A JSR-330 compliant class that is annotated with

✅ Any other class that is intended to be registered as a Spring component (i.e., a Spring bean in the ApplicationContext), potentially taking advantage of automatic autowiring of a single constructor without the use of Spring annotations.

to be added to the Environment's set of PropertySources for an Application Context for integration tests.
Attribute that specifies the location of the
The example declares that the properties are imported from the
Attributes in the form key=value that must be added to the spring environment before running the test.
Use to declare additional bean or customization for testing.
To prevent unintended conflicts, automatic registration is not possible, and it can be manually registered through

.
However, when used in the nested class, automatic registration is performed.

, an exception occurs when the Bean name overlaps.
In order to avoid this, there are cases where the same name is given and overridden.
In this case, an exception may occur and the test may fail.
In the example below, the entire
class file is overridden, so the bean named

does not exist.
An exception therefore arises.

If we specify environmental properties that allow Bean override, you can override normally.
At this time, if you register bean using
attribute, the bean will be ignored, so be careful.

This is the most powerful test annotation because it loads the same Application Context as the actual running application, allowing developers to perform any tests they want.
However, it has the disadvantage of being heavier at scale because it loads all the beans.
As in the example below, combining the above-described annotations can be made to work similarly.
Let's take a quick look at each attribute.
It is the same as the
It is the same as the
Used to specify how the embedded web server works.

It creates a Web Application Context and mocking the servlet environment without running the embedded tomcat, so you cannot make an actual request.
Therefore, it uses
for testing and is used in conjunction with
can be used in both live server and Mock environments, and only when Webflux exists in the dependency.

Create a Web Application Context and run the embedded tomcat on random port.
We can check the injected port using

for request testing.
Since the Spring runs on a thread different from the test thread,
It has the same characteristics as
except for driving the built-in tomcat with the specified port.
Create Application Context without configuring the web environment, and do not run the embedded tomcat.

Always run the main method.

or if there is no main method in that class, the execution will fail.

annotation or if there is no main method in the class, create a test-specific Spring Application.

It is the same as the use of Mock and Spy mentioned in the

.
The difference is that Mock and Spy replace the target bean and to be registered in the Application Context.
Therefore, the mock object replaces all places where the bean should have been used.
// A FooService injected with mocked FooRepository

Mock or Spy may be registered by name or type.
If there is one particular type of bean, it is registered as a type without specifying a name, but if there is more than one type of bean, it must be explicitly named using the
If the class to be Mocked is not registered as bean, it is newly registered.

Used to register component classes.
It is used in combination with annotations that do not support component class registration, such as
has a higher priority, it is first registered as bean, and then the component class loaded through

is registered as bean.
Accordingly, the component class specified in

title: Create Mocks with Mockito in Unit Test
description: Mockito is a Mocking framework that is used to create Mock objects to control situations that developers cannot control. However, if it is possible...

children.value: 💡 If you are not familiar with the terminology for test double, read
Let's find out about Spies, Stubs, Fakes, and Mocks

Mockito is a Mocking framework that is used to create Mock objects to control situations that developers cannot control.
However, if it is possible not to use the Mock object through efforts such as changing the code design, doing so can help write good code.

Mockito.mock() allows you to create a Mock object, and returns the default value for each type if you do not make any settings.
The default values are summarized as follows.
if the return value is void, nothing happens.
If the return value is of the Primitive type, it returns the default value of the primitive type.
if the return value is of the Object type, Returns
If the return value is of the Optional type, Returns
If the return value is of the Collection type, Returns empty collection.
If the way of using the annotation is convenient, it can be used.
To share between tests, the Mocks may be declared in the field to minimize redundancy.

Spies can also be created in a similar way to Mocks.
As mentioned in the article related to the test double, it acts like a real object if there is no setting.
If you use the annotation, you can only declare on the field.
Let's find out how to stub so that the Mocks can return the specified value.
Stubbing can be done by combining
💡 Since the example used in the Mocks and Stub creation chapter is used as it is, the overlapping parts will be excluded.
: Literal values can be specified according to the return type of method that enters when clause.
: Running a method that meets the conditions returns an exception.
: When a method meets the conditions, it executes a callback.
: Running a method that meets the conditions returns the execution value of the actual method.
The method of stubbing is the same as when arguments are not included, but there is a difference in that only the specified literal value argument or argument type is stubbed.
Only the corresponding literal value argument is stubbed, and using other arguments returns the default value as mentioned above.

You can specify the type to hand over to the argument.
Each matchers names are intuitive, so no explanation is required, so only examples of use are attached.
When Matchers and literal values are applied together, Matchers have priority.
is a setting that applies a loose rule, and does not cause an error even if a specified stub is not used.

In addition to the above-mentioned matchers, there are various matchers.
If you want to know about this, please refer to

Only stubbing that causes an exception is possible.
This is because the return value can be stubbed, but a compilation error occurs because the method return type is void.
Throws must be declared in the method specified to cause a Checked Exception.
Let's find out how to stub when calling the same method with the same argument.
If the same operation is performed and the literal value is different, stubbing can be performed using a varargs.
When stubbing multiple actions, perform a method chain.
calls, multiple stubbing with the same matchers or arguments is used, then each stubbing will override the previous one.
, have methods that start with do, such as

.
After that, we will call them

passes the Object type as an argument, the type safety is poor because it checks the type at runtime.
For this reason, it is recommended to use
When stubbing the void return type

when I stubbed the void in the Just the previous chapter.
Methods whose return type is void use
It is used to prevent side effects as the actual function is called when stubbing.
To verify that the specified method has been executed,
: Check how many times the specified method has been invoked. If the argument is not passed, use the default values of
: Check the method is invoked at least/most once.
: Check that at least/most the specified number of invokes has been made.
: Verify that the specified method is not invoked.
: Check that the specified Mock object has not interacted with anything.
: Check that methods are invoked in order.
💡 Even if the same method is called, it must be checked in order.
If you need more information about Verification mode, please refer to
Annotation that makes you inject both Mock and Spy objects.

title: Let's find out about Spies, Stubs, Fakes, and Mocks.
description: While searching for materials to learn about Mockito, I found a blogger calls fake object to a stub in an article about Test Double I thought all t...

children.value:
While searching for materials to learn about Mockito, I found a blogger calls fake object to a stub in an article about Test Double
I thought all the fake objects were Mocks, but I was wondering why they used the word Stub, so I looked into them.
Test double is derived from the stunt double that replaces the actor in the movie, and refers to
an object that proceeds on behalf of the object when it is difficult to proceed with the test.
In other words, it refers to an object that simplifies the components that SUT relies on so that it can focus only on the test target when testing.
Here, the test target is called SUT by shortening the System Under Test, and the component on which SUT depends is called DOC by shortening the Dependent-On Component.

props.alt: Isolation under test in unit test

children.value:
If the collaboration between SUT and DOC is not awkward, the function (behavior) is viewed as a unit and tested together is called a sociable test.
In the case of Sociable test, it is difficult to test situations that SUT cannot control, such as the current time, or code expensive to test such as Email.

Conversely, breaking the dependence of SUT and DOC and testing them on a single module basis is called a solitary test.
On the contrary, it is called solitary test to break the dependency of SUT and DOC, and test it in a single module unit.
The DOC created at this time is the test band.

💡 To describing the need for the Test Double, the sociable test was expressed somewhat negatively, but in reality, it is not.
Later, I will summarize the pros and cons of the Solitary test and Sociable test.
When you control an uncontrollable situation.

Random numbers, current time, and reading certain records from DB do not always guarantee the same results. In addition, network errors or external service conditions may not always be normal. In this way, it can be used to control situations that SUT cannot control as consistent situations.

Templates sent by e-mail, SMS, or messenger, or message contents sent to message queues are difficult to test because of the high cost of testing. In this case, the contents necessary for verification may be stored in the Test Double and then verified.
When you build an environment for creating tests.
It can be used if a specific function is not yet implemented and testing is not possible.
It is the most basic test double, which
is used only to pass over to the parameter value and is not actually used

.
This includes an object with only a default value filled in the field or an object with an empty method implementation unit.
A step up from dummies, stubs are typically

The same operation as the actual object may be performed or may be operated as a stub.
When called,
it stores the data needed for test verification
an object implemented by simplifying the operation of an actual object

, and although it is not hard-coded and operates dynamically, it is not suitable for use in a production environment.
An example is an in-memory DB using HashMap.
an object created dynamically through the Mock library

.
Depending on the configuration, it works like Dummy, Stub, or Spy.
💡 When a Spy object is created using a Mock library or testing framework,
it usually behaves like a real object and can optionally be used by stubbing the desired method.
I've checked on Mockito, Mockk, Spock, and Jest that I'm interested in, but if there's anything else, please let me know.
A mock behave like a dummy
A mock behave like a stub
A mock behave like a spy
It has low resistance to refactoring.

Low resistance to refactoring is in line with increasing code management costs.
Let's look at the example.
When using the Test double, the internal implementation of the SUT is externally exposed as follows.
At this time, if the method name or parameter of the DOC changes, in other words,
if the method signature changes, the test code must also be modified.

In the case of the Test double created manually, Since stubbing is performed outside the test, redundancy is reduced, so refactoring resistance is little less.
However, if you use the Mock library, you suffer from having to modify all the tests because you stub inside the test.

When you write stubbing, you write it expecting the DOC to actually work in production like that.
However, the real object may not work as expected.
The amount of code can be too much.
The amount of code can be too much for the reason mentioned at the end in

Although each test double was summarized as simply as possible to make it easy to distinguish, it may not be clearly distinguished when working on the actual project.
In fact, this is not unusual, and MSDN Magazine uses the following figure as an example and states that, unlike theory, the difference between each test double is actually ambiguous.
I think I can't do anything else if I focus on terms, so I'm going to focus on the role of the object.
Exploring The Continuum Of Test Doubles
What's the difference between a mock & stub?
Stub: Mocking Object For Tests In Yuk Recycle
Mock object abuse ruins the test code. - Korean
